notes.anchor: the second day of the Saturnalia
notes.note: The Saturnalia, the midwinter festival of Saturn beginning 17 December, marked by feasting, gift-giving, and a loosening of social rank; by Cicero's day it ran several days. Caesar's descent on the coast falls on its second and third days (18-19 December 45 BC).
L. Marcius Philippus, consul 56 BC, stepfather of the young Octavian and Cicero's villa-neighbour on the Bay of Naples, at whose house Caesar lodged. Cicero entertained the overflow next door.
Caesar travelled with a military escort of some two thousand soldiers, who had to be quartered and fed; the logistical burden, not the company, is what alarmed his hosts.

notes.anchor: Barba Cassius came to my aid
notes.note: Barba Cassius, a Caesarian officer who posted guards to secure Cicero's villa during the visit; otherwise little known.

Romans divided daylight into twelve hours of varying length; the seventh hour falls around midday and the eighth in the early afternoon, so Caesar worked through the morning before walking and bathing.

Mamurra, Caesar's praefectus fabrum in Gaul, who grew scandalously rich on the campaigns and was a favourite target of Catullus; the unspecified news (perhaps his death) left Caesar's expression unchanged.

A dietary practice (Greek emetike) of deliberately purging by vomiting before or between meals, which let one eat and drink freely without ill effect; Caesar's adherence to it is why he could indulge so heavily.
Baiae, the fashionable luxury resort on the Bay of Naples famed for its hot springs and seaside villas, the next stop on Caesar's itinerary after Puteoli.
Cicero reaches for the Greek term for the compulsory quartering of troops (epistathmeia) to characterize Caesar's visit: less a guest's call than an army's billet imposed on the household.
Curtius Nicias of Cos, a learned grammarian and friend of Cicero, his source for the detail of Caesar's armed escort drawing up past Dolabella's villa.
